title: OperationalDNA
description: A complete Operational DNA document for an application instance. Composes a domain hierarchy with the full behavior stack (capabilities, causes, rules, outcomes, equations) and the Actor stack (roles, users, tasks, processes). Modeled around the Actor > Action > Resource triad: Resources live inside Domains, Actions apply to Resources, and Roles (referenced by Rules, Tasks, Users, and Processes) perform Capabilities (Resource:Action pairs).

domain.description: The root domain, containing the full nested hierarchy of subdomains and resources.

capabilities.description: All Resource:Action capability pairs defined in this domain.

causes.description: What initiates each capability — user actions, schedules, webhooks, or chained capabilities.

rules.description: Constraints on capabilities — who may perform them (access rules) and what conditions must be satisfied (condition rules).

outcomes.description: State changes and side effects that result from each capability.

equations.description: Named, technology-agnostic computations that cannot be expressed as declarative Rules or Outcomes. Implemented concretely by Scripts in Technical DNA.

signals.description: Named domain events published after a Capability executes. Signals cross domain boundaries and carry typed payload contracts.

relationships.description: Named, directed connections between Resources — formalizes the semantic link a reference Attribute implies, adding cardinality.

roles.description: Roles — the Actors in the Actor > Action > Resource triad. A Role is a named job title or permission bundle (ClosingSpecialist, Underwriter, Admin, Viewer) referenced by Rules (access), Tasks (assignment), Users (who holds which role), and Processes (operator).

users.description: Named individuals or service accounts holding one or more Roles — the business directory. Documentation-grade DNA, not authentication identity.

tasks.description: Atomic reusable units of human activity: a Role performing exactly one Capability.

processes.description: Standard Operating Procedures — named, owned, ordered DAGs of Steps that accomplish business goals.
